story:
The sea keeps me prisoner with the very gift it once blessed me with. My voice rises when the tides turn and the scent of mortal life skims the water, luring innocent sailors toward their deaths. If I resist the song, the ocean floods my lungs. If I obey, I become the weapon that kills them. I was not always a monster. Once, I was a priestess—Poseidon’s Keeper of the Tides—whose dawn and dusk hymns calmed storms, guided ships, and held peace between gods and men. The waves loved me then. The people called me blessed. I believed it.

Everything changed when I met him, a fisherman with wind-tossed hair and a laugh that stills the surf. He brought me shells and stones, and once, a heart-shaped piece of sea glass carved with our initials, strung on a chain I still wear. With him, the shoreline stopped being a boundary and turned into a bridge. I tried to hide our love from Poseidon, but gods notice what mortals think they can keep. When my voice faltered and my duties slipped, the ocean roared with rage. The sea turned the color of blood, and on the wet sand a message appeared: Payment is due. One mortal soul—the one you love most—will steady the storm. Prove your loyalty, little siren.

I would not give him up. My refusal was met with divine cruelty. Poseidon took him anyway. He drowned beneath the waves I once commanded, and my grief tore itself out of me as a song I could not stop. The curse settled: my melody became a net. When the tide shifted, it dragged my voice across the horizon, and sailors walked into the sea as if sleepwalking, eyes glazed with wonder. When I tried to go silent, water clawed into my chest until I sang again. My screams braided into the music, a constant reminder of the life taken and the love lost.

Centuries wore past. Empires fell, sails became engines, maps changed, and still my voice threaded through fog and foam. Each death echoed the first. Sometimes, in the tremor of my own notes, I thought I heard him—soft, distant, answering—but when I turned, only the water watched me. Guilt gnawed my bones hollow. I sang because I had to; I hated it because I chose not to lose anyone else—until choice finally came back to me in the shape of a ship.

The sea’s hum shifted—gentler, curious. At the bow stood a young man with my love’s same smile and eyes, familiar as a heartbeat and completely new. Not him—his descendant. The song rose in my throat on instinct, and I saw it taking hold of him: the dazed gaze, the slow step toward the rail. The ocean waited, hungry. I understood the god’s game. I could kill the echo and live, or keep him safe and die. For once, I chose the ending.

I swallowed the melody. Silence burned. Water surged into my lungs, and I fell as the waves claimed me. Pain narrowed to light; the world softened to the glitter of sun through water. I did not fight. As the tide wrapped itself around me and carried me under, I thought of a boy on a deck who would walk away, and the man I once loved who could finally rest. I smiled as the sea went quiet at last.
